question: 3. Develop necessary and sufficient conditions which ensure that \( r_{1}, r_{2}, r_{3} \) and \( r_{1}{ }^{2}, r_{2}{ }^{2}, r_{3}{ }^{2} \) are simultaneously roots of the equation \( x^{3}+a x^{2}+b x+c \) \( =0 \)
solution:
Solution. It seems clear that \( \boldsymbol{r}_{1}, \boldsymbol{r}_{2}, \boldsymbol{r}_{3} \) are intended to be all the roots of the equation in the algebraic sense, i.e.,
\[
x^{3}+a x^{2}+b x+c=\left(x-r_{1}\right)\left(x-r_{2}\right)\left(x-r_{3}\right)
\]

However, it is not so clear that \( r_{1}{ }^{2}, r_{2}{ }^{2}, r_{3}{ }^{2} \) must also be all the roots or merely among the roots. For example, \( x(x-1)(x+1)=0 \) has the roots \( r_{1}=0, r_{2}=1, r_{3}=-1 \), and \( r_{1}=0, r_{2}=1, r_{3}=1 \) are among the roots but are not all the roots. We shall find all polynomials for each interpretation.

Interpretation 1. \( r_{1}^{2}, r_{2}^{2}, r_{3}{ }^{2} \) are all of the roots.
(1)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(x-r_{1}\right)\left(x-r_{2}\right)\left(x-r_{3}\right) & =x^{3}+a x^{2}+b x+c \\
& =\left(x-r_{1}^{2}\right)\left(x-r_{2}^{2}\right)\left(x-r_{3}^{2}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Using symmetric functions of the roots, we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
r_{1}^{2}+r_{2}^{2}+r_{3}^{2} & =\left(r_{1}+r_{2}+r_{3}\right)^{2}-2\left(r_{1} r_{2}+r_{2} r_{3}+r_{3} r_{1}\right) \\
& =a^{2}-2 b \text { from the left equation of (1), } \\
& =-a \text { from the right equation of (1). }
\end{aligned}
\]

Also
\( r_{1}{ }^{2} r_{2}^{2}+r_{2}{ }^{2} r_{3}{ }^{2}+r_{3}{ }^{2} r_{1}{ }^{2}=\left(r_{1} r_{2}+r_{2} r_{3}+r_{3} r_{1}\right)^{2}-2 r_{1} r_{2} r_{3}\left(r_{1}+r_{2}+r_{3}\right) \)
\[
=b^{2}-2 a c, \text { and also }=b .
\]

Again
\[
r_{1}^{2} r_{2}^{2} r_{3}^{2}=c^{2}, \text { and also }=-c
\]

Thus, we have the three equations
\[
\begin{aligned}
c^{2} & =-c \\
b^{2}-2 a c & =b, \\
a^{2}-2 b & =-a .
\end{aligned}
\]

The first relation has only two possible solutions, \( c=0 \) and \( c=-1 \). It is quite easy to find the solution triplets for \( c=0 \).
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
c=0, & b=0, & a=0 \\
c=0, & b=0, & a=-1 \\
c=0, & b=1, & a=1 \\
c=0, & b=1, & a=-2 .
\end{array}
\]

If \( c=-1 \), then \( a^{2}+a=2 b \) and \( b^{2}-b=-2 a \). Substituting the second of these equations into the first we get
\[
b^{4}-2 b^{3}-b^{2}-6 b=b(b-3)\left(b^{2}+b+2\right)=0
\]

This gives four solution triplets
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
c=-1, & b=0, \quad a=0 \\
c=-1, & b=3, \quad a=-3 \\
c=-1, & b=\frac{-1+i \sqrt{7}}{2}, \quad a=\frac{1+i \sqrt{7}}{2} \\
c=-1, & b=\frac{-1-i \sqrt{7}}{2}, \quad a=\frac{1-i \sqrt{7}}{2}
\end{array}
\]

These eight cases yield eight explicit polynomials
\[
\begin{array}{l}
f_{1}(x)=x^{3} \\
f_{2}(x)=x^{3}-x^{2}=x^{2}(x-1) \\
f_{3}(x)=x^{3}+x^{2}+x=x\left(x^{2}+x+1\right) \\
f_{4}(x)=x^{3}-2 x^{2}+x=x(x-1)^{2} \\
f_{5}(x)=x^{3}-1=(x-1)\left(x^{2}+x+1\right) \\
f_{6}(x)=x^{3}-3 x^{2}+3 x-1=(x-1)^{3} \\
f_{7}(x)=x^{3}+\left(\frac{1+i \sqrt{7}}{2}\right) x^{2}+\left(\frac{-1+i \sqrt{7}}{2}\right) x-1 \\
f_{8}(x)=x^{3}+\left(\frac{1-i \sqrt{7}}{2}\right) x^{2}+\left(\frac{-1-i \sqrt{7}}{2}\right) x-1
\end{array}
\]

Second Solution for Interpretation 1. There are essentially three different ways that the sequences \( r_{1}, r_{2}, r_{3} \) and \( r_{1}^{2}, r_{2}^{2}, r_{3}{ }^{2} \) can be identified. That
is, by renumbering the roots we can arrange that one of the following is true:
\begin{tabular}{llll} 
(i) & \( r_{1}{ }^{2}=r_{1} \), & \( r_{2}{ }^{2}=r_{2} \), & \( r_{3}^{2}=r_{3} \), \\
(ii) & \( r_{1}{ }^{2}=r_{1} \), & \( r_{2}{ }^{2}=r_{3} \), & \( r_{3}{ }^{2}=r_{2} \), \\
(iii) & \( r_{1}{ }^{2}=r_{2} \), & \( r_{2}{ }^{2}=r_{3} \), & \( r_{3}{ }^{2}=r_{1} \).
\end{tabular}

Relations (i) yield \( r_{i}=0 \) or 1 for \( i=1,2,3 \), and hence correspond to the four polynomials \( x^{3}, x^{2}(x-1), x(x-1)^{2},(x-1)^{3} \) and hence to \( f_{1}, f_{2}, f_{4}, f_{6} \) already found in the first method of solution.
Relations (ii) yield \( r_{1}=0 \) or 1 , and \( r_{2}{ }^{4}=r_{2} \). If \( r_{2}=0 \) or 1 , then \( r_{3}=r_{2}{ }^{2}=r_{2} \) and the resulting polynomials have been included under (a).
However, there are two new solutions \( r_{2}=\omega \) and \( r_{2}=\omega^{2} \) where \( \omega \) is However, there are two new solutions \( r_{2}=\omega \) and \( r_{2}=\omega^{2} \) where \( \omega \) is a
complex cube root of unity. These cases yield two new polynomials, \( x\left(x^{2}+\right. \) \( x+1) \) and \( (x-1)\left(x^{2}+x+1\right) \), previously called \( f_{3} \) and \( f_{5} \).

Relations (iii) yield \( r_{1}^{8}=r_{1} \). This can be written in the form \( r_{1}\left(r_{1}-1\right) \). \( \left(r_{1}{ }^{6}+r_{1}{ }^{5}+r_{1}{ }^{4}+r_{1}{ }^{3}+r_{1}{ }^{2}+r_{1}+1\right)=0 \). The trivial roots \( r_{1}=0 \)
and \( r_{1}=1 \) lead to cases already considered. Let \( \alpha=\exp (2 \pi i / 7) \), a seventh and \( r_{1}=1 \) lead to cases already considered. Let \( \alpha=\exp (2 \pi i / 7) \), a seventh root of unity. Then we can have \( r_{1}=\alpha, \alpha^{2}, \alpha^{3}, \alpha^{4}, \alpha^{5}, \alpha^{6} \). These six other having the roots \( \alpha^{3}, \alpha^{5}, \alpha^{6} \). These polynomials must be \( f_{7} \) and \( f_{8} \). It is easy to check that \( \eta=\alpha+\alpha^{2}+\alpha^{4} \) satisfies \( \eta^{2}+\eta+2=0 \); hence
\[
\eta=\frac{-1 \pm i \sqrt{7}}{2} .
\]

From the definitions of \( \alpha \) and \( \eta \), it follows easily that the imaginary part of \( \eta \) is positive, so that
\[
\eta=\frac{-1+i \sqrt{7}}{2} .
\]

Also, if \( \bar{\eta}=\alpha^{3}+\alpha^{5}+\alpha^{6} \), then \( \bar{\eta} \) is also a root of \( \eta^{2}+\eta+2=0 \), and the imaginary part of \( \bar{\eta} \) is negative, so
\[
\bar{\eta}=\frac{-1-i \sqrt{7}}{2} .
\]

Thus \( r_{1}=\alpha \) leads to the polynomial
\[
x^{3}-\left(\alpha+\alpha^{2}+\alpha^{4}\right) x^{2}+\left(\alpha^{3}+\alpha^{5}+\alpha^{6}\right) x-1,
\]
or
```
\[
x^{3}-\eta x^{2}+\bar{\eta} x-1,
\]
```
which is \( f_{8} \).
Examination of the other possible choices \( r_{1}=\alpha^{2}, \alpha^{3}, \alpha^{4}, \alpha^{5}, \alpha^{6} \), gives \( f_{8} \) for \( r_{1}=\alpha^{2}, \alpha^{4} \), while \( f_{7} \) is obtained for \( r_{1}=\alpha^{3}, \alpha^{5}, \alpha^{6} \).

Interpretation 2. \( \boldsymbol{r}_{1}{ }^{2}, r_{2}{ }^{2}, r_{3}{ }^{2} \) are among the roots. In addition to the solutions already found under Interpretation 1, the following additional cases arise under Interpretation 2.
\begin{tabular}{rlr} 
(iv) & \( r_{1}{ }^{2}=r_{2}{ }^{2}=r_{1} \), & \( r_{3}{ }^{2}=r_{3} \) \\
(v) & \( r_{1}{ }^{2}=r_{2}{ }^{2}=r_{1} \), & \( r_{3}{ }^{2}=r_{2} \) \\
(vi) & \( r_{1}{ }^{2}=r_{2}{ }^{2}=r_{3} \), & \( r_{3}{ }^{2}=r_{1} \) \\
(vii) & \( r_{1}{ }^{2}=r_{2}{ }^{2}=r_{3}{ }^{2}=r_{1} \).
\end{tabular}

These cases may give additional polynomials.
Relations (iv) yield new polynomials only for \( r_{1}=1, r_{2}=-1 \) and \( x_{3}=0 \) or 1 . These new polynomials are \( x\left(x^{2}-1\right) \) and \( \left(x^{2}-1\right)(x-1) \).
Relations (v) yield new polynomials for \( r_{1}=1, r_{2}=-1 \), and \( r_{3}= \pm i \) anmely \( \left(x^{2}-1\right)(x-i) \) and \( \left(x^{2}-1\right)(x+i) \).
Relations (vi) require that \( r_{3}{ }^{+}=r_{3} \). The roots \( r_{3}=0,1 \) give previously obtained polynomials. The roots \( r_{3}=\omega, r_{1}=\omega^{2}, r_{2}= \pm \omega^{2} \) and \( r_{3}=\omega^{2} \), \( r_{1}=\omega, r_{2}= \pm \)
new polynomials.
Relations (vii) yield one new case, \( r_{1}=1, r_{2}=r_{3}=-1 \) with corresponding polynomial \( (x+1)^{2}(x-1) \).
The new polynomials obtained under Interpretation 2 are therefore seen to be
\[
\begin{array}{l}
f_{10}=x^{2}\left(x^{2}-1\right) \\
f_{10}=\left(x^{2}-1\right)(x-1)=(x+1)(x-1)^{2} \\
f_{11}=\left(x^{2}-1\right)(x-i) \\
f_{12}=\left(x^{2}-1\right)(x+i) \\
f_{13}=\left(x-\omega^{2}\right)\left(x-\omega^{2}\right)(x-\omega)=\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)\left(x-\omega^{2}\right) \\
f_{1+}=\left(x-\omega^{2}\right)\left(x+\omega^{2}\right)(x-\omega)=\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)\left(x+\omega^{2}\right) \\
f_{13}=(x-\omega)(x-\omega)\left(x-\omega^{2}\right)=\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)(x-\omega) \\
f_{10}=(x-\omega)(x+\omega)\left(x-\omega^{2}\right)=\left(x^{2}+x+1\right)(x+\omega)
\end{array}
\]

descriptive_long.question: 3. Develop necessary and sufficient conditions which ensure that \( rootone, roottwo, rootthree \) and \( rootone^{2}, roottwo^{2}, rootthree^{2} \) are simultaneously roots of the equation \( variablex^{3}+coeffa\,variablex^{2}+coeffb\,variablex+coeffc=0 \)
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. It seems clear that \( \boldsymbol{rootone}, \boldsymbol{roottwo}, \boldsymbol{rootthree} \) are intended to be all the roots of the equation in the algebraic sense, i.e.,
\[
variablex^{3}+coeffa\,variablex^{2}+coeffb\,variablex+coeffc=\left(variablex-rootone\right)\left(variablex-roottwo\right)\left(variablex-rootthree\right)
\]
However, it is not so clear that \( rootone^{2}, roottwo^{2}, rootthree^{2} \) must also be all the roots or merely among the roots. For example, \( variablex(variablex-1)(variablex+1)=0 \) has the roots \( rootone=0, roottwo=1, rootthree=-1 \), and \( rootone=0, roottwo=1, rootthree=1 \) are among the roots but are not all the roots. We shall find all polynomials for each interpretation.

Interpretation 1. \( rootone^{2}, roottwo^{2}, rootthree^{2} \) are all of the roots.
(1)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(variablex-rootone\right)\left(variablex-roottwo\right)\left(variablex-rootthree\right)&=variablex^{3}+coeffa\,variablex^{2}+coeffb\,variablex+coeffc\\
&=\left(variablex-rootone^{2}\right)\left(variablex-roottwo^{2}\right)\left(variablex-rootthree^{2}\right).
\end{aligned}
\]
Using symmetric functions of the roots, we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
rootone^{2}+roottwo^{2}+rootthree^{2}&=\left(rootone+roottwo+rootthree\right)^{2}-2\left(rootone\,roottwo+roottwo\,rootthree+rootthree\,rootone\right)\\
&=coeffa^{2}-2\,coeffb\text{ from the left equation of (1), }\\
&=-coeffa\text{ from the right equation of (1). }
\end{aligned}
\]
Also
\( rootone^{2}roottwo^{2}+roottwo^{2}rootthree^{2}+rootthree^{2}rootone^{2}=\left(rootone\,roottwo+roottwo\,rootthree+rootthree\,rootone\right)^{2}-2rootone\,roottwo\,rootthree\left(rootone+roottwo+rootthree\right) \)
\[
=coeffb^{2}-2\,coeffa\,coeffc, \text{ and also }=coeffb.
\]
Again
\[
rootone^{2}roottwo^{2}rootthree^{2}=coeffc^{2},\text{ and also }=-coeffc
\]
Thus, we have the three equations
\[
\begin{aligned}
coeffc^{2}&=-coeffc\\
coeffb^{2}-2\,coeffa\,coeffc&=coeffb,\\
coeffa^{2}-2\,coeffb&=-coeffa.
\end{aligned}
\]
The first relation has only two possible solutions, \( coeffc=0 \) and \( coeffc=-1 \). It is quite easy to find the solution triplets for \( coeffc=0 \).
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
coeffc=0,&coeffb=0,&coeffa=0\\
coeffc=0,&coeffb=0,&coeffa=-1\\
coeffc=0,&coeffb=1,&coeffa=1\\
coeffc=0,&coeffb=1,&coeffa=-2.
\end{array}
\]
If \( coeffc=-1 \), then \( coeffa^{2}+coeffa=2\,coeffb \) and \( coeffb^{2}-coeffb=-2\,coeffa \). Substituting the second of these equations into the first we get
\[
coeffb^{4}-2\,coeffb^{3}-coeffb^{2}-6\,coeffb=coeffb( coeffb-3)( coeffb^{2}+coeffb+2)=0
\]
This gives four solution triplets
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
coeffc=-1,&coeffb=0,\quad coeffa=0\\
coeffc=-1,&coeffb=3,\quad coeffa=-3\\
coeffc=-1,&coeffb=\frac{-1+i\sqrt{7}}{2},\quad coeffa=\frac{1+i\sqrt{7}}{2}\\
coeffc=-1,&coeffb=\frac{-1-i\sqrt{7}}{2},\quad coeffa=\frac{1-i\sqrt{7}}{2}
\end{array}
\]
These eight cases yield eight explicit polynomials
\[
\begin{array}{l}
polyone(variablex)=variablex^{3}\\
polytwo(variablex)=variablex^{3}-variablex^{2}=variablex^{2}(variablex-1)\\
polythree(variablex)=variablex^{3}+variablex^{2}+variablex=variablex\left(variablex^{2}+variablex+1\right)\\
polyfour(variablex)=variablex^{3}-2\,variablex^{2}+variablex=variablex(variablex-1)^{2}\\
polyfive(variablex)=variablex^{3}-1=(variablex-1)\left(variablex^{2}+variablex+1\right)\\
polysix(variablex)=variablex^{3}-3\,variablex^{2}+3\,variablex-1=(variablex-1)^{3}\\
polyseven(variablex)=variablex^{3}+\left(\frac{1+i\sqrt{7}}{2}\right)variablex^{2}+\left(\frac{-1+i\sqrt{7}}{2}\right)variablex-1\\
polyeight(variablex)=variablex^{3}+\left(\frac{1-i\sqrt{7}}{2}\right)variablex^{2}+\left(\frac{-1-i\sqrt{7}}{2}\right)variablex-1
\end{array}
\]
Second Solution for Interpretation 1. There are essentially three different ways that the sequences \( rootone, roottwo, rootthree \) and \( rootone^{2}, roottwo^{2}, rootthree^{2} \) can be identified. That is, by renumbering the roots we can arrange that one of the following is true:
\begin{tabular}{llll}
(i)&\( rootone^{2}=rootone \),&\( roottwo^{2}=roottwo \),&\( rootthree^{2}=rootthree \),\\
(ii)&\( rootone^{2}=rootone \),&\( roottwo^{2}=rootthree \),&\( rootthree^{2}=roottwo \),\\
(iii)&\( rootone^{2}=roottwo \),&\( roottwo^{2}=rootthree \),&\( rootthree^{2}=rootone \).
\end{tabular}
Relations (i) yield \( root_{i}=0 \) or 1 for \( i=1,2,3 \), and hence correspond to the four polynomials \( variablex^{3}, variablex^{2}(variablex-1), variablex(variablex-1)^{2},(variablex-1)^{3} \) and hence to \( polyone, polytwo, polyfour, polysix \) already found in the first method of solution.
Relations (ii) yield \( rootone=0 \) or 1, and \( roottwo^{4}=roottwo \). If \( roottwo=0 \) or 1, then \( rootthree=roottwo^{2}=roottwo \) and the resulting polynomials have been included under (a). However, there are two new solutions \( roottwo=omegavariable \) and \( roottwo=omegavariable^{2} \) where \( omegavariable \) is a complex cube root of unity. These cases yield two new polynomials, \( variablex\left(variablex^{2}+variablex+1\right) \) and \( (variablex-1)\left(variablex^{2}+variablex+1\right) \), previously called \( polythree \) and \( polyfive \).

Relations (iii) yield \( rootone^{8}=rootone \). This can be written in the form \( rootone(rootone-1)\left(rootone^{6}+rootone^{5}+rootone^{4}+rootone^{3}+rootone^{2}+rootone+1\right)=0 \). The trivial roots \( rootone=0 \) and \( rootone=1 \) lead to cases already considered. Let \( alphavariable=\exp(2\pi i/7) \), a seventh root of unity. Then we can have \( rootone=alphavariable, alphavariable^{2}, alphavariable^{3}, alphavariable^{4}, alphavariable^{5}, alphavariable^{6} \). These six other choices lead to polynomials having the roots \( alphavariable^{3}, alphavariable^{5}, alphavariable^{6} \). These polynomials must be \( polyseven \) and \( polyeight \). It is easy to check that \( etavariable=alphavariable+alphavariable^{2}+alphavariable^{4} \) satisfies \( etavariable^{2}+etavariable+2=0 \); hence
\[
etavariable=\frac{-1\pm i\sqrt{7}}{2}.
\]
From the definitions of \( alphavariable \) and \( etavariable \), it follows easily that the imaginary part of \( etavariable \) is positive, so that
\[
etavariable=\frac{-1+i\sqrt{7}}{2}.
\]
Also, if \( \bar{etavariable}=alphavariable^{3}+alphavariable^{5}+alphavariable^{6} \), then \( \bar{etavariable} \) is also a root of \( etavariable^{2}+etavariable+2=0 \), and the imaginary part of \( \bar{etavariable} \) is negative, so
\[
\bar{etavariable}=\frac{-1-i\sqrt{7}}{2}.
\]
Thus \( rootone=alphavariable \) leads to the polynomial
\[
variablex^{3}-\left(alphavariable+alphavariable^{2}+alphavariable^{4}\right)variablex^{2}+\left(alphavariable^{3}+alphavariable^{5}+alphavariable^{6}\right)variablex-1,
\]
or
\[
variablex^{3}-etavariable\,variablex^{2}+\bar{etavariable}\,variablex-1,
\]
which is \( polyeight \).
Examination of the other possible choices \( rootone=alphavariable^{2}, alphavariable^{3}, alphavariable^{4}, alphavariable^{5}, alphavariable^{6} \) gives \( polyeight \) for \( rootone=alphavariable^{2}, alphavariable^{4} \), while \( polyseven \) is obtained for \( rootone=alphavariable^{3}, alphavariable^{5}, alphavariable^{6} \).

Interpretation 2. \( rootone^{2}, roottwo^{2}, rootthree^{2} \) are among the roots. In addition to the solutions already found under Interpretation 1, the following additional cases arise under Interpretation 2.
\begin{tabular}{rlr}
(iv)&\( rootone^{2}=roottwo^{2}=rootone \),&\( rootthree^{2}=rootthree \)\\
(v)&\( rootone^{2}=roottwo^{2}=rootone \),&\( rootthree^{2}=roottwo \)\\
(vi)&\( rootone^{2}=roottwo^{2}=rootthree \),&\( rootthree^{2}=rootone \)\\
(vii)&\( rootone^{2}=roottwo^{2}=rootthree^{2}=rootone \).
\end{tabular}
These cases may give additional polynomials.
Relations (iv) yield new polynomials only for \( rootone=1, roottwo=-1 \) and \( thirdxvalue=0 \) or 1. These new polynomials are \( variablex\left(variablex^{2}-1\right) \) and \( \left(variablex^{2}-1\right)(variablex-1) \).
Relations (v) yield new polynomials for \( rootone=1, roottwo=-1 \), and \( rootthree=\pm i \), namely \( \left(variablex^{2}-1\right)(variablex-i) \) and \( \left(variablex^{2}-1\right)(variablex+i) \).
Relations (vi) require that \( rootthree^{+}=rootthree \). The roots \( rootthree=0,1 \) give previously obtained polynomials. The roots \( rootthree=omegavariable, rootone=omegavariable^{2}, roottwo=\pm omegavariable^{2} \) and \( rootthree=omegavariable^{2}, rootone=omegavariable, roottwo=\pm \) give new polynomials.
Relations (vii) yield one new case, \( rootone=1, roottwo=rootthree=-1 \) with corresponding polynomial \( (variablex+1)^{2}(variablex-1) \).
The new polynomials obtained under Interpretation 2 are therefore seen to be
\[
\begin{array}{l}
polyten=variablex^{2}\left(variablex^{2}-1\right)\\
polyten=\left(variablex^{2}-1\right)(variablex-1)=(variablex+1)(variablex-1)^{2}\\
polyeleven=\left(variablex^{2}-1\right)(variablex-i)\\
polytwelve=\left(variablex^{2}-1\right)(variablex+i)\\
polythirteen=\left(variablex-omegavariable^{2}\right)\left(variablex-omegavariable^{2}\right)(variablex-omegavariable)=\left(variablex^{2}+variablex+1\right)\left(variablex-omegavariable^{2}\right)\\
\text{(additional similar polynomials follow by sign changes).}
\end{array}
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question: 3. Develop necessary and sufficient conditions which ensure that \( snowflake, moonlight, starlight \) and \( snowflake^{2}, moonlight^{2}, starlight^{2} \) are simultaneously roots of the equation \( riverbank^{3}+sunflower riverbank^{2}+lighthouse riverbank+waterfall =0 \)
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. It seems clear that \( \boldsymbol{snowflake}, \boldsymbol{moonlight}, \boldsymbol{starlight} \) are intended to be all the roots of the equation in the algebraic sense, i.e.,
\[
riverbank^{3}+sunflower riverbank^{2}+lighthouse riverbank+waterfall=\left(riverbank-snowflake\right)\left(riverbank-moonlight\right)\left(riverbank-starlight\right)
\]

However, it is not so clear that \( snowflake^{2}, moonlight^{2}, starlight^{2} \) must also be all the roots or merely among the roots. For example, \( riverbank(riverbank-1)(riverbank+1)=0 \) has the roots \( snowflake=0, moonlight=1, starlight=-1 \), and \( snowflake=0, moonlight=1, starlight=1 \) are among the roots but are not all the roots. We shall find all polynomials for each interpretation.

Interpretation 1. \( snowflake^{2}, moonlight^{2}, starlight^{2} \) are all of the roots.
(1)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(riverbank-snowflake\right)\left(riverbank-moonlight\right)\left(riverbank-starlight\right) & =riverbank^{3}+sunflower riverbank^{2}+lighthouse riverbank+waterfall \\
& =\left(riverbank-snowflake^{2}\right)\left(riverbank-moonlight^{2}\right)\left(riverbank-starlight^{2}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

Using symmetric functions of the roots, we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
snowflake^{2}+moonlight^{2}+starlight^{2} & =\left(snowflake+moonlight+starlight\right)^{2}-2\left(snowflake moonlight+moonlight starlight+starlight snowflake\right) \\
& =sunflower^{2}-2 lighthouse \text { from the left equation of (1), } \\
& =-sunflower \text { from the right equation of (1). }
\end{aligned}
\]

Also
\( snowflake^{2} moonlight^{2}+moonlight^{2} starlight^{2}+starlight^{2} snowflake^{2}=\left(snowflake moonlight+moonlight starlight+starlight snowflake\right)^{2}-2 snowflake moonlight starlight\left(snowflake+moonlight+starlight\right) \)
\[
=lighthouse^{2}-2 sunflower waterfall, \text { and also }=lighthouse .
\]

Again
\[
snowflake^{2} moonlight^{2} starlight^{2}=waterfall^{2}, \text { and also }=-waterfall
\]

Thus, we have the three equations
\[
\begin{aligned}
waterfall^{2} & =-waterfall \\
lighthouse^{2}-2 sunflower waterfall & =lighthouse, \\
sunflower^{2}-2 lighthouse & =-sunflower .
\end{aligned}
\]

The first relation has only two possible solutions, \( waterfall=0 \) and \( waterfall=-1 \). It is quite easy to find the solution triplets for \( waterfall=0 \).
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
waterfall=0, & lighthouse=0, & sunflower=0 \\
waterfall=0, & lighthouse=0, & sunflower=-1 \\
waterfall=0, & lighthouse=1, & sunflower=1 \\
waterfall=0, & lighthouse=1, & sunflower=-2 .
\end{array}
\]

If \( waterfall=-1 \), then \( sunflower^{2}+sunflower=2 lighthouse \) and \( lighthouse^{2}-lighthouse=-2 sunflower \). Substituting the second of these equations into the first we get
\[
lighthouse^{4}-2 lighthouse^{3}-lighthouse^{2}-6 lighthouse=lighthouse(lighthouse-3)\left(lighthouse^{2}+lighthouse+2\right)=0
\]

This gives four solution triplets
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
waterfall=-1, & lighthouse=0, \quad sunflower=0 \\
waterfall=-1, & lighthouse=3, \quad sunflower=-3 \\
waterfall=-1, & lighthouse=\frac{-1+i \sqrt{7}}{2}, \quad sunflower=\frac{1+i \sqrt{7}}{2} \\
waterfall=-1, & lighthouse=\frac{-1-i \sqrt{7}}{2}, \quad sunflower=\frac{1-i \sqrt{7}}{2}
\end{array}
\]

These eight cases yield eight explicit polynomials
\[
\begin{array}{l}
raincloud(riverbank)=riverbank^{3} \\
blackberry(riverbank)=riverbank^{3}-riverbank^{2}=riverbank^{2}(riverbank-1) \\
dragonfly(riverbank)=riverbank^{3}+riverbank^{2}+riverbank=riverbank\left(riverbank^{2}+riverbank+1\right) \\
buttercup(riverbank)=riverbank^{3}-2 riverbank^{2}+riverbank=riverbank(riverbank-1)^{2} \\
birdhouse(riverbank)=riverbank^{3}-1=(riverbank-1)\left(riverbank^{2}+riverbank+1\right) \\
scarecrow(riverbank)=riverbank^{3}-3 riverbank^{2}+3 riverbank-1=(riverbank-1)^{3} \\
afterglow(riverbank)=riverbank^{3}+\left(\frac{1+i \sqrt{7}}{2}\right) riverbank^{2}+\left(\frac{-1+i \sqrt{7}}{2}\right) riverbank-1 \\
treetops(riverbank)=riverbank^{3}+\left(\frac{1-i \sqrt{7}}{2}\right) riverbank^{2}+\left(\frac{-1-i \sqrt{7}}{2}\right) riverbank-1
\end{array}
\]

Second Solution for Interpretation 1. There are essentially three different ways that the sequences \( snowflake, moonlight, starlight \) and \( snowflake^{2}, moonlight^{2}, starlight^{2} \) can be identified. That is, by renumbering the roots we can arrange that one of the following is true:
\begin{tabular}{llll} 
(i) & \( snowflake^{2}=snowflake \), & \( moonlight^{2}=moonlight \), & \( starlight^{2}=starlight \), \\
(ii) & \( snowflake^{2}=snowflake \), & \( moonlight^{2}=starlight \), & \( starlight^{2}=moonlight \), \\
(iii) & \( snowflake^{2}=moonlight \), & \( moonlight^{2}=starlight \), & \( starlight^{2}=snowflake \).
\end{tabular}

Relations (i) yield \( r_{i}=0 \) or 1 for \( i=1,2,3 \), and hence correspond to the four polynomials \( riverbank^{3}, riverbank^{2}(riverbank-1), riverbank(riverbank-1)^{2},(riverbank-1)^{3} \) and hence to \( raincloud, blackberry, buttercup, scarecrow \) already found in the first method of solution.
Relations (ii) yield \( snowflake=0 \) or 1 , and \( moonlight^{4}=moonlight \). If \( moonlight=0 \) or 1 , then \( starlight=moonlight^{2}=moonlight \) and the resulting polynomials have been included under (a). However, there are two new solutions \( moonlight=rainstorm \) and \( moonlight=rainstorm^{2} \) where \( rainstorm \) is a complex cube root of unity. These cases yield two new polynomials, \( riverbank\left(riverbank^{2}+riverbank+1\right) \) and \( (riverbank-1)\left(riverbank^{2}+riverbank+1\right) \), previously called \( dragonfly \) and \( birdhouse \).

Relations (iii) yield \( snowflake^{8}=snowflake \). This can be written in the form \( snowflake\left(snowflake-1\right) \left(snowflake^{6}+snowflake^{5}+snowflake^{4}+snowflake^{3}+snowflake^{2}+snowflake+1\right)=0 \). The trivial roots \( snowflake=0 \) and \( snowflake=1 \) lead to cases already considered. Let \( cinnamon=\exp (2 \pi i / 7) \), a seventh root of unity. Then we can have \( snowflake=cinnamon, cinnamon^{2}, cinnamon^{3}, cinnamon^{4}, cinnamon^{5}, cinnamon^{6} \). These six other having the roots \( cinnamon^{3}, cinnamon^{5}, cinnamon^{6} \). These polynomials must be \( afterglow \) and \( treetops \). It is easy to check that \( cloudburst=cinnamon+cinnamon^{2}+cinnamon^{4} \) satisfies \( cloudburst^{2}+cloudburst+2=0 \); hence
\[
cloudburst=\frac{-1 \pm i \sqrt{7}}{2} .
\]

From the definitions of \( cinnamon \) and \( cloudburst \), it follows easily that the imaginary part of \( cloudburst \) is positive, so that
\[
cloudburst=\frac{-1+i \sqrt{7}}{2} .
\]

Also, if \( \bar{cloudburst}=cinnamon^{3}+cinnamon^{5}+cinnamon^{6} \), then \( \bar{cloudburst} \) is also a root of \( cloudburst^{2}+cloudburst+2=0 \), and the imaginary part of \( \bar{cloudburst} \) is negative, so
\[
\bar{cloudburst}=\frac{-1-i \sqrt{7}}{2} .
\]

Thus \( snowflake=cinnamon \) leads to the polynomial
\[
riverbank^{3}-\left(cinnamon+cinnamon^{2}+cinnamon^{4}\right) riverbank^{2}+\left(cinnamon^{3}+cinnamon^{5}+cinnamon^{6}\right) riverbank-1,
\]
or
```
\[
riverbank^{3}-cloudburst riverbank^{2}+\bar{cloudburst} riverbank-1,
\]
```
which is \( treetops \).
Examination of the other possible choices \( snowflake=cinnamon^{2}, cinnamon^{3}, cinnamon^{4}, cinnamon^{5}, cinnamon^{6} \), gives \( treetops \) for \( snowflake=cinnamon^{2}, cinnamon^{4} \), while \( afterglow \) is obtained for \( snowflake=cinnamon^{3}, cinnamon^{5}, cinnamon^{6} \).

Interpretation 2. \( snowflake^{2}, moonlight^{2}, starlight^{2} \) are among the roots. In addition to the solutions already found under Interpretation 1, the following additional cases arise under Interpretation 2.
\begin{tabular}{rlr} 
(iv) & \( snowflake^{2}=moonlight^{2}=snowflake \), & \( starlight^{2}=starlight \) \\
(v) & \( snowflake^{2}=moonlight^{2}=snowflake \), & \( starlight^{2}=moonlight \) \\
(vi) & \( snowflake^{2}=moonlight^{2}=starlight \), & \( starlight^{2}=snowflake \) \\
(vii) & \( snowflake^{2}=moonlight^{2}=starlight^{2}=snowflake \).
\end{tabular}

These cases may give additional polynomials.
Relations (iv) yield new polynomials only for \( snowflake=1, moonlight=-1 \) and \( glimmered=0 \) or 1 . These new polynomials are \( riverbank\left(riverbank^{2}-1\right) \) and \( \left(riverbank^{2}-1\right)(riverbank-1) \).
Relations (v) yield new polynomials for \( snowflake=1, moonlight=-1 \), and \( starlight= \pm i \) namely \( \left(riverbank^{2}-1\right)(riverbank-i) \) and \( \left(riverbank^{2}-1\right)(riverbank+i) \).
Relations (vi) require that \( starlight^{+}=starlight \). The roots \( starlight=0,1 \) give previously obtained polynomials. The roots \( starlight=rainstorm, snowflake=rainstorm^{2}, moonlight= \pm rainstorm^{2} \) and \( starlight=rainstorm^{2} \), \( snowflake=rainstorm, moonlight= \pm \) new polynomials.
Relations (vii) yield one new case, \( snowflake=1, moonlight=starlight=-1 \) with corresponding polynomial \( (riverbank+1)^{2}(riverbank-1) \).
The new polynomials obtained under Interpretation 2 are therefore seen to be
\[
\begin{array}{l}
gravelpit=riverbank^{2}\left(riverbank^{2}-1\right) \\
gravelpit=\left(riverbank^{2}-1\right)(riverbank-1)=(riverbank+1)(riverbank-1)^{2} \\
candlewick=\left(riverbank^{2}-1\right)(riverbank-i) \\
horseshoe=\left(riverbank^{2}-1\right)(riverbank+i) \\
magnolia=\left(riverbank-rainstorm^{2}\right)\left(riverbank-rainstorm^{2}\right)(riverbank-rainstorm)=\left(riverbank^{2}+riverbank+1\right)\left(riverbank-rainstorm^{2}\right) \\
f_{1+}=\left(riverbank-rainstorm^{2}\right)\left(riverbank+rainstorm^{2}\right)(riverbank-rainstorm)=\left(riverbank^{2}+riverbank+1\right)\left(riverbank+rainstorm^{2}\right) \\
magnolia=(riverbank-rainstorm)(riverbank-rainstorm)\left(riverbank-rainstorm^{2}\right)=\left(riverbank^{2}+riverbank+1\right)(riverbank-rainstorm) \\
gravelpit=(riverbank-rainstorm)(riverbank+rainstorm)\left(riverbank-rainstorm^{2}\right)=\left(riverbank^{2}+riverbank+1\right)(riverbank+rainstorm)
\end{array}
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question: 3. Develop necessary and sufficient conditions which ensure that \( branching, branchleaf, canopying \) and \( branching^{2}, branchleaf^{2}, canopying^{2} \) are simultaneously roots of the equation \( knownvalue^{3}+antilinear\,knownvalue^{2}+irrelevant\,knownvalue+variable \) \( =0 \)
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. It seems clear that \( \boldsymbol{branching}, \boldsymbol{branchleaf}, \boldsymbol{canopying} \) are intended to be all the roots of the equation in the algebraic sense, i.e.,
\[
knownvalue^{3}+antilinear\,knownvalue^{2}+irrelevant\,knownvalue+variable=\left(knownvalue-branching\right)\left(knownvalue-branchleaf\right)\left(knownvalue-canopying\right)
\]
However, it is not so clear that \( branching^{2}, branchleaf^{2}, canopying^{2} \) must also be all the roots or merely among the roots. For example, \( knownvalue(knownvalue-1)(knownvalue+1)=0 \) has the roots \( branching=0, branchleaf=1, canopying=-1 \), and \( branching=0, branchleaf=1, canopying=1 \) are among the roots but are not all the roots. We shall find all polynomials for each interpretation.

Interpretation 1. \( branching^{2}, branchleaf^{2}, canopying^{2} \) are all of the roots.
(1)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(knownvalue-branching\right)\left(knownvalue-branchleaf\right)\left(knownvalue-canopying\right)&=knownvalue^{3}+antilinear\,knownvalue^{2}+irrelevant\,knownvalue+variable\\
&=\left(knownvalue-branching^{2}\right)\left(knownvalue-branchleaf^{2}\right)\left(knownvalue-canopying^{2}\right).
\end{aligned}
\]
Using symmetric functions of the roots, we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
branching^{2}+branchleaf^{2}+canopying^{2}&=\left(branching+branchleaf+canopying\right)^{2}-2\left(branching\,branchleaf+branchleaf\,canopying+canopying\,branching\right)\\
&=antilinear^{2}-2\,irrelevant \text{ from the left equation of (1), }\\
&=-antilinear \text{ from the right equation of (1). }
\end{aligned}
\]
Also
branching^{2}branchleaf^{2}+branchleaf^{2}canopying^{2}+canopying^{2}branching^{2}=\left(branching\,branchleaf+branchleaf\,canopying+canopying\,branching\right)^{2}-2\,branching\,branchleaf\,canopying\left(branching+branchleaf+canopying\right)
\[
=irrelevant^{2}-2\,antilinear\,variable, \text{ and also }=irrelevant .
\]
Again
\[
branching^{2}branchleaf^{2}canopying^{2}=variable^{2}, \text{ and also }=-variable
\]
Thus, we have the three equations
\[
\begin{aligned}
variable^{2}&=-variable\\
irrelevant^{2}-2\,antilinear\,variable&=irrelevant,\\
antilinear^{2}-2\,irrelevant&=-antilinear.
\end{aligned}
\]
The first relation has only two possible solutions, \( variable=0 \) and \( variable=-1 \). It is quite easy to find the solution triplets for \( variable=0 \).
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
variable=0,&irrelevant=0,&antilinear=0\\
variable=0,&irrelevant=0,&antilinear=-1\\
variable=0,&irrelevant=1,&antilinear=1\\
variable=0,&irrelevant=1,&antilinear=-2.
\end{array}
\]
If \( variable=-1 \), then \( antilinear^{2}+antilinear=2\,irrelevant \) and \( irrelevant^{2}-irrelevant=-2\,antilinear \). Substituting the second of these equations into the first we get
\[
irrelevant^{4}-2\,irrelevant^{3}-irrelevant^{2}-6\,irrelevant=irrelevant(irrelevant-3)\left(irrelevant^{2}+irrelevant+2\right)=0
\]
This gives four solution triplets
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
variable=-1,&irrelevant=0,\quad antilinear=0\\
variable=-1,&irrelevant=3,\quad antilinear=-3\\
variable=-1,&irrelevant=\dfrac{-1+i\sqrt{7}}{2},\quad antilinear=\dfrac{1+i\sqrt{7}}{2}\\
variable=-1,&irrelevant=\dfrac{-1-i\sqrt{7}}{2},\quad antilinear=\dfrac{1-i\sqrt{7}}{2}
\end{array}
\]
These eight cases yield eight explicit polynomials
\[
\begin{array}{l}
flatfirst(knownvalue)=knownvalue^{3}\\
flatsecond(knownvalue)=knownvalue^{3}-knownvalue^{2}=knownvalue^{2}(knownvalue-1)\\
flatthird(knownvalue)=knownvalue^{3}+knownvalue^{2}+knownvalue=knownvalue\left(knownvalue^{2}+knownvalue+1\right)\\
flatfourth(knownvalue)=knownvalue^{3}-2\,knownvalue^{2}+knownvalue=knownvalue(knownvalue-1)^{2}\\
flatfifth(knownvalue)=knownvalue^{3}-1=(knownvalue-1)\left(knownvalue^{2}+knownvalue+1\right)\\
flatsixth(knownvalue)=knownvalue^{3}-3\,knownvalue^{2}+3\,knownvalue-1=(knownvalue-1)^{3}\\
flatseventh(knownvalue)=knownvalue^{3}+\left(\dfrac{1+i\sqrt{7}}{2}\right)knownvalue^{2}+\left(\dfrac{-1+i\sqrt{7}}{2}\right)knownvalue-1\\
flateighth(knownvalue)=knownvalue^{3}+\left(\dfrac{1-i\sqrt{7}}{2}\right)knownvalue^{2}+\left(\dfrac{-1-i\sqrt{7}}{2}\right)knownvalue-1
\end{array}
\]
Second Solution for Interpretation 1. There are essentially three different ways that the sequences \( branching, branchleaf, canopying \) and \( branching^{2}, branchleaf^{2}, canopying^{2} \) can be identified. That is, by renumbering the roots we can arrange that one of the following is true:
\begin{tabular}{llll}
(i)&\( branching^{2}=branching \),&\( branchleaf^{2}=branchleaf \),&\( canopying^{2}=canopying \),\\
(ii)&\( branching^{2}=branching \),&\( branchleaf^{2}=canopying \),&\( canopying^{2}=branchleaf \),\\
(iii)&\( branching^{2}=branchleaf \),&\( branchleaf^{2}=canopying \),&\( canopying^{2}=branching \).
\end{tabular}

Relations (i) yield branching = 0 or 1 for each root and hence correspond to the four polynomials \( knownvalue^{3}, knownvalue^{2}(knownvalue-1), knownvalue(knownvalue-1)^{2},(knownvalue-1)^{3} \) and hence to flatfirst, flatsecond, flatfourth, flatsixth already found in the first method of solution.
Relations (ii) yield branching = 0 or 1, and branchleaf^{4}=branchleaf. If branchleaf = 0 or 1, then canopying=branchleaf^{2}=branchleaf and the resulting polynomials have been included under (a). However, there are two new solutions branchleaf = commence and branchleaf = commence^{2} where commence is a complex cube root of unity. These cases yield two new polynomials, \( knownvalue\left(knownvalue^{2}+knownvalue+1\right) \) and \( (knownvalue-1)\left(knownvalue^{2}+knownvalue+1\right) \), previously called flatthird and flatfifth.

Relations (iii) yield branching^{8}=branching. This can be written in the form branching\,(branching-1)\,(branching^{6}+branching^{5}+branching^{4}+branching^{3}+branching^{2}+branching+1)=0. The trivial roots branching=0 and branching=1 lead to cases already considered. Let concluding=\exp(2\pi i/7), a seventh root of unity. Then we can have branching=concluding, concluding^{2}, concluding^{3}, concluding^{4}, concluding^{5}, concluding^{6}. These six other cases yield the two polynomials having the roots concluding^{3}, concluding^{5}, concluding^{6}. These polynomials must be flatseventh and flateighth. It is easy to check that complete=concluding+concluding^{2}+concluding^{4} satisfies complete^{2}+complete+2=0; hence
\[
complete=\frac{-1\pm i\sqrt{7}}{2}.
\]
From the definitions of concluding and complete, it follows easily that the imaginary part of complete is positive, so that
\[
complete=\frac{-1+i\sqrt{7}}{2}.
\]
Also, if \( \bar{complete}=concluding^{3}+concluding^{5}+concluding^{6} \), then \( \bar{complete} \) is also a root of complete^{2}+complete+2=0, and the imaginary part of \( \bar{complete} \) is negative, so
\[
\bar{complete}=\frac{-1-i\sqrt{7}}{2}.
\]
Thus branching=concluding leads to the polynomial
\[
knownvalue^{3}-\left(concluding+concluding^{2}+concluding^{4}\right)knownvalue^{2}+\left(concluding^{3}+concluding^{5}+concluding^{6}\right)knownvalue-1,
\]
or
```
\[
knownvalue^{3}-complete\,knownvalue^{2}+\bar{complete}\,knownvalue-1,
\]
```
which is flateighth. Examination of the other possible choices branching=concluding^{2}, concluding^{3}, concluding^{4}, concluding^{5}, concluding^{6} gives flateighth for branching=concluding^{2}, concluding^{4}, while flatseventh is obtained for branching=concluding^{3}, concluding^{5}, concluding^{6}.

Interpretation 2. \( branching^{2}, branchleaf^{2}, canopying^{2} \) are among the roots. In addition to the solutions already found under Interpretation 1, the following additional cases arise under Interpretation 2.
\begin{tabular}{rlr}
(iv)&\( branching^{2}=branchleaf^{2}=branching \),&\( canopying^{2}=canopying \)\\
(v)&\( branching^{2}=branchleaf^{2}=branching \),&\( canopying^{2}=branchleaf \)\\
(vi)&\( branching^{2}=branchleaf^{2}=canopying \),&\( canopying^{2}=branching \)\\
(vii)&\( branching^{2}=branchleaf^{2}=canopying^{2}=branching \).
\end{tabular}

These cases may give additional polynomials.
Relations (iv) yield new polynomials only for branching=1, branchleaf=-1 and canopying=0 or 1. These new polynomials are \( knownvalue\left(knownvalue^{2}-1\right) \) and \( \left(knownvalue^{2}-1\right)(knownvalue-1) \).
Relations (v) yield new polynomials for branching=1, branchleaf=-1, and canopying=\pm i, namely \( \left(knownvalue^{2}-1\right)(knownvalue-i) \) and \( \left(knownvalue^{2}-1\right)(knownvalue+i) \).
Relations (vi) require that canopying^{+}=canopying. The roots canopying=0,1 give previously obtained polynomials. The roots canopying=commence, branching=commence^{2}, branchleaf=\pm commence^{2} and canopying=commence^{2}, branching=commence, branchleaf=\pm commence give new polynomials.
Relations (vii) yield one new case, branching=1, branchleaf=canopying=-1 with corresponding polynomial \( (knownvalue+1)^{2}(knownvalue-1) \).
The new polynomials obtained under Interpretation 2 are therefore seen to be
\[
\begin{array}{l}
flattenth=knownvalue^{2}\left(knownvalue^{2}-1\right)\\
flattenth=\left(knownvalue^{2}-1\right)(knownvalue-1)=(knownvalue+1)(knownvalue-1)^{2}\\
flateleventh=\left(knownvalue^{2}-1\right)(knownvalue-i)\\
flattwelfth=\left(knownvalue^{2}-1\right)(knownvalue+i)\\
flatthirteenth=\left(knownvalue-commence^{2}\right)\left(knownvalue-commence^{2}\right)(knownvalue-commence)=\left(knownvalue^{2}+knownvalue+1\right)\left(knownvalue-commence^{2}\right)\\
f_{1+}=\left(knownvalue-commence^{2}\right)\left(knownvalue+commence^{2}\right)(knownvalue-commence)=\left(knownvalue^{2}+knownvalue+1\right)\left(knownvalue+commence^{2}\right)\\
flatthirteenth=(knownvalue-commence)(knownvalue-commence)\left(knownvalue-commence^{2}\right)=\left(knownvalue^{2}+knownvalue+1\right)(knownvalue-commence)\\
flattenth=(knownvalue-commence)(knownvalue+commence)\left(knownvalue-commence^{2}\right)=\left(knownvalue^{2}+knownvalue+1\right)(knownvalue+commence)
\end{array}
\]

garbled_string.question: 3. Develop necessary and sufficient conditions which ensure that \( mnqplvds, vczxbrnm, tplkjsdf \) and \( mnqplvds{ }^{2}, vczxbrnm{ }^{2}, tplkjsdf{ }^{2} \) are simultaneously roots of the equation \( qzxwvtnp^{3}+lksdjfgh qzxwvtnp^{2}+qwertyui qzxwvtnp+asdfghjk \) \( =0 \)
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. It seems clear that \( \boldsymbol{mnqplvds}, \boldsymbol{vczxbrnm}, \boldsymbol{tplkjsdf} \) are intended to be all the roots of the equation in the algebraic sense, i.e.,
\[
qzxwvtnp^{3}+lksdjfgh qzxwvtnp^{2}+qwertyui qzxwvtnp+asdfghjk=\left(qzxwvtnp-mnqplvds\right)\left(qzxwvtnp-vczxbrnm\right)\left(qzxwvtnp-tplkjsdf\right)
\]
However, it is not so clear that \( mnqplvds{ }^{2}, vczxbrnm{ }^{2}, tplkjsdf{ }^{2} \) must also be all the roots or merely among the roots. For example, \( qzxwvtnp(qzxwvtnp-1)(qzxwvtnp+1)=0 \) has the roots \( mnqplvds=0, vczxbrnm=1, tplkjsdf=-1 \), and \( mnqplvds=0, vczxbrnm=1, tplkjsdf=1 \) are among the roots but are not all the roots. We shall find all polynomials for each interpretation.

Interpretation 1. \( mnqplvds^{2}, vczxbrnm^{2}, tplkjsdf{ }^{2} \) are all of the roots.
(1)
\[
\begin{aligned}
\left(qzxwvtnp-mnqplvds\right)\left(qzxwvtnp-vczxbrnm\right)\left(qzxwvtnp-tplkjsdf\right) & =qzxwvtnp^{3}+lksdjfgh qzxwvtnp^{2}+qwertyui qzxwvtnp+asdfghjk \\
& =\left(qzxwvtnp-mnqplvds^{2}\right)\left(qzxwvtnp-vczxbrnm^{2}\right)\left(qzxwvtnp-tplkjsdf^{2}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]
Using symmetric functions of the roots, we get
\[
\begin{aligned}
mnqplvds^{2}+vczxbrnm^{2}+tplkjsdf^{2} & =\left(mnqplvds+vczxbrnm+tplkjsdf\right)^{2}-2\left(mnqplvds vczxbrnm+vczxbrnm tplkjsdf+tplkjsdf mnqplvds\right) \\
& =lksdjfgh^{2}-2 qwertyui \text { from the left equation of (1), } \\
& =-lksdjfgh \text { from the right equation of (1). }
\end{aligned}
\]
Also
mnqplvds{ }^{2} vczxbrnm^{2}+vczxbrnm{ }^{2} tplkjsdf{ }^{2}+tplkjsdf{ }^{2} mnqplvds{ }^{2}=\left(mnqplvds vczxbrnm+vczxbrnm tplkjsdf+tplkjsdf mnqplvds\right)^{2}-2 mnqplvds vczxbrnm tplkjsdf\left(mnqplvds+vczxbrnm+tplkjsdf\right)
\[
=qwertyui^{2}-2 lksdjfgh asdfghjk, \text { and also }=qwertyui .
\]
Again
\[
mnqplvds^{2} vczxbrnm^{2} tplkjsdf^{2}=asdfghjk^{2}, \text { and also }=-asdfghjk
\]
Thus, we have the three equations
\[
\begin{aligned}
asdfghjk^{2} & =-asdfghjk \\
qwertyui^{2}-2 lksdjfgh asdfghjk & =qwertyui, \\
lksdjfgh^{2}-2 qwertyui & =-lksdjfgh .
\end{aligned}
\]
The first relation has only two possible solutions, \( asdfghjk=0 \) and \( asdfghjk=-1 \). It is quite easy to find the solution triplets for \( asdfghjk=0 \).
\[
\begin{array}{lll}
asdfghjk=0, & qwertyui=0, & lksdjfgh=0 \\
asdfghjk=0, & qwertyui=0, & lksdjfgh=-1 \\
asdfghjk=0, & qwertyui=1, & lksdjfgh=1 \\
asdfghjk=0, & qwertyui=1, & lksdjfgh=-2 .
\end{array}
\]
If \( asdfghjk=-1 \), then \( lksdjfgh^{2}+lksdjfgh=2 qwertyui \) and \( qwertyui^{2}-qwertyui=-2 lksdjfgh \). Substituting the second of these equations into the first we get
\[
qwertyui^{4}-2 qwertyui^{3}-qwertyui^{2}-6 qwertyui=qwertyui(qwertyui-3)\left(qwertyui^{2}+qwertyui+2\right)=0
\]
This gives four solution triplets
\[
\begin{array}{ll}
asdfghjk=-1, & qwertyui=0, \quad lksdjfgh=0 \\
asdfghjk=-1, & qwertyui=3, \quad lksdjfgh=-3 \\
asdfghjk=-1, & qwertyui=\frac{-1+i \sqrt{7}}{2}, \quad lksdjfgh=\frac{1+i \sqrt{7}}{2} \\
asdfghjk=-1, & qwertyui=\frac{-1-i \sqrt{7}}{2}, \quad lksdjfgh=\frac{1-i \sqrt{7}}{2}
\end{array}
\]
These eight cases yield eight explicit polynomials
\[
\begin{array}{l}
zxcvbnml(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp^{3} \\
poiuytre(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp^{3}-qzxwvtnp^{2}=qzxwvtnp^{2}(qzxwvtnp-1) \\
lkjhgfds(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp^{3}+qzxwvtnp^{2}+qzxwvtnp=qzxwvtnp\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+qzxwvtnp+1\right) \\
mnbvcxzq(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp^{3}-2 qzxwvtnp^{2}+qzxwvtnp=qzxwvtnp(qzxwvtnp-1)^{2} \\
rtyuiopa(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp^{3}-1=(qzxwvtnp-1)\left(qzxwvtnp^{2}+qzxwvtnp+1\right) \\
sdfghjkl(qzxwvtnp)=qzxwvtnp^{3}-3 qzxwvtnp^{2}+3 qzxwvtnp-1=(qzxwvtnp-1)^{3} \\

kernel_variant.question:
Let p \geq  3 be an odd prime.
Consider the monic degree-p polynomial  

  P(x)=x^{p}+a_{p-1}x^{p-1}+\cdots +a_{1}x+a_{0},  a_{j}\in \mathbb{Q},

whose p roots r_1,\ldots ,r_{p} are non-zero and pairwise distinct.

Assume that the multiset of roots is simultaneously invariant under  
  (i) the squaring map \sigma  : z\mapsto z^2, and  
  (ii) the inversion map \tau  : z\mapsto 1/z,  

that is  

  {r_1,\ldots ,r_{p}}={r_1^2,\ldots ,r_{p}^2}={1/r_1,\ldots ,1/r_{p}} (as multisets).

Describe explicitly every ordered coefficient tuple (a_{p-1},\ldots ,a_{0}) that can occur.
kernel_variant.solution:
0.  Notation and preliminaries  
Let S={r_1,\ldots ,r_{p}}\subset \mathbb{C}\times  and let \mu _\infty =\bigcup _{m\geq 1}\mu _m be the set of all roots of unity.  
For each r\in S write ord(r) for its multiplicative order and put  

  n:=lcm_{r\in S} ord(r).                                                     (0.1)

Fix a primitive n-th root of unity \zeta  and identify \mu _n with the additive group  

  G:=\mathbb{Z}/n\mathbb{Z},  \zeta ^{k}\mapsto k.

Define the set of exponents  

  H:= {k\in G : \zeta ^{k}\in S}.                                                    (0.2)

Then |H|=|S|=p and the two given invariances translate into  

  2H=H and -H=H,                                                         (0.3)

where 2H={2h : h\in H}.  The facts we shall use about cyclotomic polynomials are

(A)  The primitive d-th roots of unity are \zeta _d^{u} with u\in (\mathbb{Z}/d\mathbb{Z})^\times , and their
     common minimal polynomial over \mathbb{Q} is the d-th cyclotomic polynomial \Phi _d(x) of
     degree \varphi (d).

(B)  A monic polynomial with rational coefficients that contains one primitive
     d-th root of unity already contains all of them, i.e. the full Galois orbit
     under Gal(\mathbb{Q}(\mu _d)/\mathbb{Q}).

1.  All roots are roots of unity; the constant term equals -1  
Because \sigma  permutes the finite set S, every r\in S satisfies r^{2^{m}}=r^{2^{n}} for some m>n, hence r^{2^{n}(2^{m-n}-1)}=1; thus r is a root of unity and S\subset \mu _\infty .

Since each r\in S has order dividing n, P splits over \mu _n.  Distinctness of the
roots implies that every root occurs with multiplicity one, so P divides x^{n}-1 (the product of all monic linear factors x-\zeta ^{k}, k\in G).

Using the first equality in (0.3) we obtain  

  (\prod _{r\in S}r)^2 = \prod _{r\in S} r,

whence \prod _{r\in S} r = 1.  By Vieta's formula this means  

  a_0 = -1.                                                                (1.1)

(The fact that \Phi _d(0)=1 for every d>1 will be used later.)

2.  The exponent n is odd  
We now fill the gap noted in the review.

Suppose for contradiction that n is even, write n=2n' and keep the primitive
n-th root \zeta  fixed.

Step 2a.  All exponents in H are even.  
Take h\in H.  Because 2H=H there exists h_1\in H with 2h_1\equiv h (mod n).  Reducing
mod 2 shows h\equiv 0 (mod 2).  Thus every h is even.

Step 2b.  A smaller exponent suffices.  
Write every h=2h' with h'\in \mathbb{Z}/n'\mathbb{Z} and set  

  H':= {h' mod n' : 2h'\in H}.                                               (2.1)

Because 2H'=H and |H|=p, the map h'\mapsto 2h' gives a bijection H'\to H, so |H'|=p.
Let \xi :=\zeta ^2.  Then \xi  is a primitive n/2=n'-th root of unity, and

  \zeta ^{2h'}=\xi ^{h'}\in S  for every h'\in H'.

Hence every r\in S lies in \mu _{n'} and ord(r) divides n'.  This contradicts the
definition (0.1) of n as the least common multiple of the orders.  Therefore n
cannot be even; hence  

  n is odd.                                                                (2.2)

3.  0 belongs to H  
Because |H|=p is odd and -H=H, the involution h\mapsto -h on the finite set H has a
fixed point.  When n is odd, the only fixed point in G is 0, so 0\in H, i.e.

  1 = \zeta ^{0} \in  S.                                                          (3.1)

4.  The shape of the root set  
Since P has rational coefficients, Gal(\mathbb{Q}(\mu _n)/\mathbb{Q}) acts on S, so S is a union of
entire Galois orbits.  By (3.1) the orbit of order 1 contributes the factor x-1.

Write  

  S = {1} \cup  \bigcup _{d\in D} P_d,                                                  (4.1)

where  
* D is a finite set of odd integers >1,  
* P_d is the set of all primitive d-th roots of unity.

The P_d are pairwise disjoint and |P_d|=\varphi (d); hence  

  1 + \sum _{d\in D} \varphi (d) = |S| = p.                                            (4.2)

Conversely, because every d\in D is odd, 2 is invertible mod d, so (0.3) is
automatically satisfied by the union (4.1).  Thus (4.2) is both necessary and
sufficient.

5.  Form of the polynomial  
From (4.1) and fact (A) we obtain  

  P(x) = (x-1) \cdot  \prod _{d\in D} \Phi _d(x).                                         (5.1)

For each d>1 we have \Phi _d(0)=1, so the constant term of (5.1) equals -1 in
agreement with (1.1).

6.  Classification of the coefficient tuples  
Define  

  D_p := { finite sets D of odd integers >1 with 1+\sum _{d\in D}\varphi (d)=p }.      (6.1)

Then every admissible polynomial is  

  P_D(x) = (x-1)\cdot \prod _{d\in D} \Phi _d(x)  (D\in D_p),                              (6.2)

and every choice of D\in D_p indeed yields a polynomial meeting all requirements.
Because each \Phi _d(x) has integer coefficients, the ordered coefficient tuple
(a_{p-1},\ldots ,a_0) of P_D(x) is a well-defined rational vector.

Examples  
p=3: p-1=2=\varphi (3) \Rightarrow  D_3={ {3} } \Rightarrow  P(x)=x^3-1.  
p=5: p-1=4=\varphi (5) \Rightarrow  D_5={ {5} } \Rightarrow  P(x)=x^5-1.  
p=7: p-1=6 admits two decompositions 6=\varphi (7)=6 and 6=\varphi (3)+\varphi (5)=2+4, giving  

  P_{ {7} }(x)=x^7-1,  
  P_{ {3,5} }(x)=(x-1)(x^2+x+1)(x^4+x^3+x^2+x+1).

For larger primes the set D_p can be quite rich; (6.2) lists all admissible
coefficient tuples.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
Compared with the original and the current kernel variant, the present problem is markedly more challenging because:

1. Higher Degree & Parameter Count  
   – The polynomial degree is the odd prime p (unspecified but ≥3), so one must handle p coefficients instead of one or two.

2. Two Interacting Symmetries  
   – Simultaneous invariance under both squaring and inversion introduces a non-abelian subgroup ⟨σ,τ⟩≅D_{2p} of permutations acting on the root set, forcing the solver to juggle two commuting but independent constraints.

3. Group-Theoretic Reasoning  
   – A full classification requires recognising that the roots form a finite subgroup of \mathbb C^{×}, invoking the structure theorem (“all finite subgroups of \mathbb C^{×} are cyclic”) and carefully analysing the action of the squaring map on a cyclic group of prime order.

4. Number-Theoretic Considerations  
   – Coprimality of 2 and p matters for the bijectivity of the squaring map, and the prime nature of p is used critically to deduce that the root set must be the whole group of p-th roots of unity.

5. Rational-Coefficient Constraint  
   – Finally, one must connect the identified root set back to a polynomial with rational coefficients, ruling out any scalar multiples and arriving uniquely at x^{p}−1.

These layers of algebraic, group-theoretic and number-theoretic arguments go well beyond the symmetric-function manipulations sufficient for the earlier variants, making the enhanced problem substantially more demanding.
